name: Migrate State Between Backends
description: Move Terraform state from local to remote backend (S3, GCS, Azure) safely for team collaboration and disaster recovery.
trendingReason: Teams migrating from local state to remote backends as infrastructure scales beyond single-developer workflows.

steps.title: Back up the current state file
steps.description: Create a local backup of your state file before making any changes to the backend configuration.
steps.tip: Always keep a backup. If the migration fails, you can restore from this copy.

steps.title: Add the remote backend configuration
steps.description: Define the new backend block in your Terraform configuration pointing to the remote storage location.
steps.tip: Use a DynamoDB table for state locking to prevent concurrent modifications.

steps.title: Initialize with migration flag
steps.description: Run terraform init with the -migrate-state flag. Terraform detects the backend change and prompts to copy existing state.
steps.warning: Answer 'yes' when prompted. If you answer 'no', your state will not be migrated and you may lose track of managed resources.

steps.title: Verify state in remote backend
steps.description: List all resources to confirm the state was migrated correctly and all resources are accounted for.

steps.title: Run a plan to confirm no changes
steps.description: Execute a plan to ensure Terraform sees no differences between state and infrastructure after migration.
steps.tip: A clean plan with 0 changes confirms the migration was successful.

steps.title: Remove the local state file
steps.description: Delete the local terraform.tfstate now that state lives in the remote backend. Keep the backup for a few days.
steps.warning: Only delete local state after you have confirmed the remote backend is working correctly.

fullScript:
#!/bin/bash
set -euo pipefail

# Terraform State Migration Script
# Migrates local state to an S3 remote backend

STATE_BUCKET="${1:?Usage: $0 <bucket-name> <dynamodb-table> <region>}"
LOCK_TABLE="${2:?Usage: $0 <bucket-name> <dynamodb-table> <region>}"
REGION="${3:-us-east-1}"

echo "==> Backing up current state..."
cp terraform.tfstate terraform.tfstate.backup 2>/dev/null || echo "No local state file found"

echo "==> Writing backend configuration..."
cat > backend.tf <<EOF
terraform {
  backend "s3" {
    bucket         = "$STATE_BUCKET"
    key            = "prod/terraform.tfstate"
    region         = "$REGION"
    dynamodb_table = "$LOCK_TABLE"
    encrypt        = true
  }
}
EOF

echo "==> Initializing with migration..."
terraform init -migrate-state -input=false

echo "==> Verifying state..."
terraform state list

echo "==> Running plan to confirm no drift..."
terraform plan -detailed-exitcode || {
  if [ $? -eq 2 ]; then
    echo "WARNING: Plan shows changes. Review before proceeding."
    exit 1
  fi
}

echo "==> Migration complete. Remote backend is active."
echo "    Backup saved at terraform.tfstate.backup"

faq.question: Can I migrate state back to local?
faq.answer: Yes. Remove the backend block from your configuration and run 'terraform init -migrate-state'. Terraform will prompt to copy state back to a local file.

faq.question: What happens if the migration is interrupted midway?
faq.answer: Terraform writes state atomically. If interrupted, the state remains in the original backend. Restore your backup and try again.

faq.question: Do I need a DynamoDB table for S3 backend?
faq.answer: It is strongly recommended. The DynamoDB table provides state locking which prevents two people from applying changes at the same time, avoiding state corruption.
